coredata.dc:title: Rapid recognition of di-n-butyl phthalate in food samples with a near infrared fluorescence imprinted sensor based on zeolite imidazolate framework-67
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shan
Fu, Jinli
Zhou, Shu
Zhao, Pengfei
Wu, Xiaodan
Tang, Sisi
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Di-n-butyl phthalate (DBP) as a plasticizer is widely used in food and chemical industries. It is harm to human health when it appeared in food and water. A novel near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on CdTe quantum dots and zeolite imidazolate framework-67 was developed with a sol–gel polymerization method for rapid and sensitive determination of DBP in foodstuff rapidly (only in 1.5 min). The fluorescence imprinted sensor provided a rapid detection method for DBP in the linear response concentration range of 0.05–18.0 μM with a low detection limit of 1.6 nM. Compared with previous fluorescence imprinted sensor, it behaved faster response speed and lower detection limit for determination of DBP. The fluorescence imprinted sensor was used to detect DBP in real samples successfully with satisfied recoveries of 97.2–106.4%, suggesting a potential application in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer
Di-n-butyl phthalate
Zeolite imidazolate framework-67
Near infrared
Fluorescence quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP, CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP and CdTe@MIP
Effect of pH and incubation time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of ZIF-67 (a), CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (b), CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP (c). TEM images of CdTe QDs (d), ZIF-67 (e), CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (f). XRD patterns of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP (g) (insert, ZIF-67). EDS spectra of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (h) and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP (i).

Effects of pH on fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (a), effects of pH on fluorescence quenching of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP (b), effects of fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP at different incubation times (c), effect of the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@MIP toward DBP at different incubation times (d). The data are displayed as the mean ± SD for n = 3. Each group with different subscripts is significantly different (p < 0.05).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (a) and CdTe @ZIF-67@NIP (b) with addition of certain concentration of DBP. Stern-Volmer plots for CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (c) and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP (d) with DBP.
Determination results of DBP in water and food samples with the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor.
Performance comparison of different methods for the determination of DBP.

head.ce:title: Rapid recognition of di-n-butyl phthalate in food samples with a near infrared fluorescence imprinted sensor based on zeolite imidazolate framework-67

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Di-n-butyl phthalate (DBP) as a plasticizer is widely used in food and chemical industries. It is harm to human health when it appeared in food and water. A novel near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on CdTe quantum dots and zeolite imidazolate framework-67 was developed with a sol–gel polymerization method for rapid and sensitive determination of DBP in foodstuff rapidly (only in 1.5 min). The fluorescence imprinted sensor provided a rapid detection method for DBP in the linear response concentration range of 0.05–18.0 μM with a low detection limit of 1.6 nM. Compared with previous fluorescence imprinted sensor, it behaved faster response speed and lower detection limit for determination of DBP. The fluorescence imprinted sensor was used to detect DBP in real samples successfully with satisfied recoveries of 97.2–106.4%, suggesting a potential application in food analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Di-n-butyl phthalate (DBP), one of phthalate esters, was widely used in polyvinyl chloride plastics to improve their flexibility and plasticity (Silva et al., 2007). However, DBP in food packaging bags, water pipes or plastic waste were leaked into food, water and ecosystem environment easily (Xiang et al., 2019; Ostrovský et al., 2011). Moreover, previous studies claimed that DBP is a kind of endocrine disrupting compound causing cancer and reducing fertility when it accumulates in organisms (Yue, Huang, Shen, Yang, & Pang, 2020; Dolai, Ali, & Jana, 2020), which has been classified as priority hazardous substances. According to previous report (Zhu et al., 2019), the maximum allowable limit of DBP in drinking water was 3 ng∙mL−1 (0.01 μM). In addition, the found level of DBP reached up to 61 μg∙L-1 (0.22 μM) in nine rivers and two seas in China, which could be enriched in aquatic organisms (Qu, Feng, Sun, & Wang, 2015). Currently, many methods were developed for the determination of DBP (Zhang, Luo, Cai, & Chen, 2013; Blount et al., 2000; Xu et al., 2017; Liang et al., 2017). Among of them, fluorescence analysis method for determining DBP was selective and sensitive but time-consuming.

Molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) is a class of macromolecule polymers that have many special recognition cavities matching with template in structure, shape and size (Li et al., 2018). Fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIP) are a series of polymers combing the outstanding selectivity of MIP and the excellent sensitivity of fluorescence detection (Cai, Zhang, Xiao, Chen, & Fu, 2020). Up to now, FMIPs has been applied for the determination of DBP. Li et al prepared a FMIP based on Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (QDs) for detecting DBP within 10 min in a linear range of 5.0–50.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.27 μM (Li et al., 2016), which was used to detect DBP in tap water samples. Xu et al added magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the Mn-doped ZnS QDs based FMIP to improve its sensitivity, which could detect DBP in a linear range of 5.0–50.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.08 μM. It was applied to detect DBP in real brand distilled spirit samples, but the response time of the magnetic FMIP determining DBP rose to 21 min (Xu et al., 2017). Therefore, it is urgent to develop a sensitive, selective and rapid method for the determination of DBP.

Near infrared (NIR) fluorescence QDs (650–900 nm) with high sensitivity, size tunable fluorescence, good chemical and photo stabilities have been reported in recent years (Liu, Zhang, Qiao, & Su, 2012; Yan et al., 2019). They have been widely used for determining, sensing and bio-imaging. For instance, Cao et al developed a ratiometric fluorescence method based on CdSe@ZnS QDs for detecting mercuric ion (Cao et al., 2013). Liang et al proposed a Mn/CdTe QDs based fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensor for recognizing human IgG (Liang et al., 2009). Gu et al reported a tunable Ag2Se QDs for imaging in vivo (Gu, Cui, Zhang, Xie, & Pang, 2012). The previous reports indicated the NIR fluorescence QDs can improve the sensitivity of FMIP.

Zeolite imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) have many advantages including the numerous structures, tunable pore sizes, chemical stability and high specific surface areas. When ZIFs were introduced into FMIP, they can improve the specific surface area of FMIP and shorten the fluorescence response time of FMIP (Cao, Hu, Zhao, Mao, & Fang, 2021). Zeolite imidazolate framework-67 (ZIF-67) with adjustable structure and high specific surface area was formed via packaging central metal atom (Co2+) with organic ligand (2-methylimidazole) (Li, Jin, & Zhao, 2020), which have been prevalent in catalyst (Qin, Wang, & Wang, 2017), adsorption (Du et al., 2017) and sensor (Xu et al., 2019).

This work reported a NIR FMIPs sensor based on CdTe QDs and ZIF-67 for the sensitive and selective determination of DBP rapidly. The CdTe QDs were synthesized by one-pot reaction. Meanwhile, ZIF-67 was prepared by a facile method under mild conditions. The MIP layer was covered on the surface of CdTe QDs and ZIF-67 by sol–gel polymerization. ZIF-67 with high specific surface areas could improve the fluorescence response sensitivity and response speed of the FMIP based sensor for the determination of DBP. The outstanding sensitivity and selectivity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP provided a novel method for rapid determining DBP in food.

Dioctyl phthalate (DOP, 99%), dibutyl phthalate (DBP, 99%), diethyl phthalate (DEP, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98%), 2-methylimidazole (HmIM, 98%), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES, 98%), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 99%), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25%), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (H4ClNO, 98%), ammonium acetate (CH3COONH4, 98%), o-chlorophenol (o-CP, 99%), o-nitrophenol (o-NP, 99%), ortho-nitroaniline (o-NA, 99%) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Tellurium powder (Te, 99%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98%), cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 98%) and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 99%) were purchased from Kermel (Tianjin, China). Absolute methanol and ethanol were bought from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). All inorganic salts in experience were gained from Kermel (Tianjin, China).

Fluorescence intensities of materials were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The morphologies of materials were severally characterized on JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, Japan) and Sigma HD scanning electron microscope (SEM; Carl Zeiss AG, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the prepared materials were carried on an IR Affinisy-1 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Ultraviolet absorption spectra of materials were operated on a UV-1780 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Energy dispersive spectra (EDS) analysis of materials was obtained from an X-MaxN instrument (Oxford Instruments, England). Ultrasonic operation was carried on KQ-250E ultrasonic cleaner with a power of 250 W brought from Kunshan Ultrasonic Instruments Co., Ltd (Kunshan, China). All centrifugal operations were executed on a Neofuge 15 high speed centrifuge instrument brought from Shanghai Lishen Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Magnetic stirring operation was operated on 79–1 magnetic heating stirrer gained from Jiangsu Zhongda Instruments Technology Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China).

CdTe QDs was synthesized according to the literature with some modifications (Zou et al., 2008). Briefly, 30.8 mg of NaBH4 and 6.0 mg of Te powder were blown with nitrogen in a glass centrifuge tube for 5 min. Then 10 mL of nitrogen saturated double distilled water was added. The reaction system was treated with ultrasound for 1 h under anaerobic conditions to obtain colorless NaHTe solution. Additionally, 53.2 mg of CdCl2·2.5H2O and 40 μL of MPA were dispersed into 40 mL of distilled water in a three-necked flask. Afterwards, the pH value of this mixture solution was adjusted to 12.0 by 1 M NaOH to obtain the precursor solution. Then the fresh NaHTe solution was poured into the precursor solution fleetly under anaerobic condition, and the mixture solution was reacted in a water bath at 100 °C for 50 min. When the fluorescence emission peak of the reaction solution was measured at 695 nm under an excited wavelength of 400 nm, the synthesized CdTe QDs solution was placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The preparation of ZIF-67 particles was referred to Yang’s work (Yang et al., 2018). Firstly, 12.5 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 25.0 mL of methanol to obtain cobalt-methanol precursor solution. After that, 50.0 mmol of HmIM was dissolved in 25.0 mL of methanol to prepare the HmIM solution. Then, the cobalt-methanol precursor solution and HmIM solution were mixed at room temperature and stirred for 60 min. After the reaction solution was stood for 12 h, it was centrifuged at 8400 g for 5 min, and the residue was washed with ethanol for three times. Finally, the obtained purple ZIF-67 was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP, CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP and CdTe@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was polymerized by sol–gel method (Cai, Zhang, Xiao, Chen, & Fu, 2019). Firstly, 10 mg of DBP, 2 mg of ZIF-67, 100 μL of APTES and 5 mL of CdTe QDs solution were added into 10 mL of ethanol. After this solution was magnetic stirred with 500 rpm at room temperature for 15 min, 60 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of NH3·H2O were added. After that, the mixture solution was stirred for 12 h. Finally, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 4700 g for 5 min, and the residue was washed with ethanol for 3 times. The prepared CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was dried in 50 °C vacuum oven for 10 h. The non-imprinted polymer based on CdTe QDs and ZIF-67 (CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP) was synthesized by the same process without adding DBP. The MIP based on CdTe QDs (CdTe@MIP) was prepared by the similar process without adding ZIF-67.

Some ions including Cu2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Na+, Mn2+, NO3 –, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cl-, Ag+, SO4 2- and K+ were selected as background interfering ions for the anti-interference experiment. Firstly, 1.5 mg of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was dispersed into 5 mL of distilled water. Then 0.5 μM of DBP and certain concentration ions solution were added into the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP solution (pH = 7.0). After incubated for 1.5 min, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured. Fluorescence intensity measure was carried out on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer with excitation wavelength of 400 nm, slit width of 10 nm and voltage of 600 V. All fluorescence measurements were replicated three times and the average was calculated to analysis.

Furthermore, o-CP, o-NP, o-NA, DOP and DEP were chosen as competitive reagents for the selectivity experience of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP. These competitors’ concentrations were set as 0.5 μM. The experiment procedure was similar as the ions interference experiment.

The practicality of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was implemented in several food samples involving water, fish, milk and juice. Tap water and barrelled water were obtained from laboratory. Bottled water, fish, milk and juice were brought from local supermarket. Water samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane to remove of suspended particles. Fish sample was prepared according to the previous report (Wu, Lin, Zhong, Chen, & Huang, 2017). Briefly, 5.0 g of skinless fish muscle tissue was dispersed in a mixture solution of hydroxylamine hydrochloride (20%, 1.5 mL) and ammonium acetate solution (pH = 4.5, 3.5 mL) at room temperature for 12 h. After centrifuged at 8400 g for 5 min, it was washed with PBS buffer solution (pH = 7). The liquid supernatant was collected and placed at 4 °C for further use. Milk sample was prepared according to Farajzadeh’s work (Farajzadeh, Djozan, Reza, Mogaddam, & Norouzi, 2012). Briefly, 1.0 g of NaCl was dissolved in 10 mL of milk for 5 min. After 6 mL of acetonitrile was added, the biomacromolecule was removed by centrifuging at 2100 g for 5 min. Then, the substratum liquid was collected and diluted to 100-fold. Juice sample was prepared on the basis of Shui’s report (Shui & Leong, 2002). 10 mL of juice was centrifuged at 3300 g for 10 min, and the liquid supernatant was diluted to 100 times. 1.5 mg of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was dispersed to 5 mL of real sample solutions. After incubated for 1.5 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 695 nm. All fluorescence measurements were replicated three times and the average value was calculated to analysis.

The statistical analysis was executed by analysis variance (ANOVA) according to the SAS User’s Guide, and the significant differences (p < 0.05) between the means were estimated by Duncan’s multiple range test.

This work reported a novel fluorescence imprinted polymer for the rapid determination of DBP selectively and sensitively by combining the molecular imprinting technology with NIR quantum dots and ZIF-67. As shown in Fig. 1 , the CdTe QDs with emission wavelength of 695 nm were prepared by one-pot solvothermal method. The carboxyl groups of MPA attached onto the surface of CdTe nanoparticles, which exhibited strong NIR fluorescence. ZIF-67 with high specific surface area prepared by a facile method was introduced into the MIP to enhance the rate of mass transfer and binding capacity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (Xu, Pan, Fang, & Wang, 2019). Thus, the carboxyl groups on the NIR CdTe QDs cooperated with amino groups on ZIF-67 surface. After that, the pre-polymers of the template molecules pre-polymerized with functional monomers were covered to the surface of ZIF-67 through cross-linking. Finally, the specific recognition cavities were formed after removed of DBP. Fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was quenched extremely when DBP molecule was rebound, which also can be recovered once DBP was removed from the imprinted sites.

The morphologies of the materials were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the image of the ZIF-67 microcrystals was classic rhombic dodecahedron with an average size of 650 nm, which was conformed to previous studies indicating that the ZIF-67 was prepared successfully (Zhang, Cai, & Liu, 2019). After polymerized, the average size of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP shown in Fig. 2b and c was about 850 nm, which was larger than that of ZIF-67. Additionally, the TEM image shown in Fig. 2d exhibited the diameter of CdTe QDs was about 4.8 nm. The TEM image of ZIF-67 shown in Fig. 2e exhibited that the ZIF-67 with uniform size was successfully synthesized. The TEM image of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP in Fig. 2f showed that the shell of the imprinted layer with thickness of 90 nm was coated on the surface of ZIF-67, which indicated that the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was prepared successfully.

The XRD patterns of ZIF-67, CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP were shown in Fig. 2g. The XRD patterns of ZIF-67 shown in the inset graph agreed with previous reports (Xu et al., 2019), which exhibited the successful synthesis of ZIF-67. The XRD patterns of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP were different from ZIF-67 indicated their crystallinities were changed obviously after imprinted. These data suggested the successful preparation of molecularly imprinted polymer layer.

In addition, the EDS analysis of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP were shown in Fig. 2h and i. For CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP, the weight percentage of C, O, Si, S, Co, Cd and Te were 46.77%, 33.45%, 12.47%, 0.84%, 4.77%, 1.52% and 0.18%, respectively. For CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP, the weight percentages of C, O, Si, S, Co, Cd and Te were 48.85%, 23.30%, 7.91%, 3.11%, 9.50%, 7.21% and 0.12%, respectively. The subsistent of Co indicated the successful introduction of ZIF-67, and the existents of S, Cd and Te elements suggested the successful preparation of CdTe QDs. The appearance of Si element manifested the successful preparation of imprinted layer.

Fig.S1 presented the FT-IR spectra of ZIF-67 (curve a), CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (curve b) and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP (curve c). The wide absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl. The peak at 3170 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CH groups (Gao et al., 2018). The absorption peaks at 1400 cm-1and 1303 cm−1 were ascribed to the CN stretching vibration of imidazole ring of HmIM. And the peak at 1170 cm−1 was contributed to the flexural vibration of imidazole ring of HmIM. These data indicated the successful synthesis of ZIF-67 (Zhong et al., 2019). The peaks at 1050 cm−1 and 770 cm−1 showed the appearance of Si-O-Si stretching vibration and Si-O stretching vibration, which derived from APTES and TEOS (Li, Liu, & Ren, 2017). Above data indicated that the MIP layer was successfully coated onto the surface of ZIF-67.

As shown in Fig.S2, there was no overlap existed between the UV/vis absorption spectrum of DBP and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CdTe QDs, which suggested that the fluorescence quenching was not attributed with the energy transfer mechanism in this report (Zhang & Chen, 2016). Additionally, the UV/vis spectra of DBP, CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs@DBP in Fig.S3 displayed no non-fluorescence complex created between DBP and CdTe QDs, excluding the possibility of static quenching (Cai et al., 2019). A temperature-dependent experiment of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP was executed at different temperatures. As shown in Fig.S4, the fluorescence quenching constant increased as the temperature growing indicating the fluorescence quenching phenomenon between CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and DBP attributed to dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and incubation time
ce:section.ce:para: The solution pH might influence the fluorescence response of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP and the form of template molecule in the solution. Fig. 3 a shows the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP at different pH values (p < 0.05). The maximal fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP at pH 7 was observed. While the pH value was lower or higher than pH 7, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually. This phenomenon resulted from the dissolution of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP at lower pH and the ionization of the silica shell of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP at higher pH (Li, Li, & Cheng, 2010). Moreover, the fluorescence quenching of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP at different pH values were shown in Fig. 3b. The binding affinity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP increased as pH value growing and behaved an optimum at pH 7, after then it decreased gradually (p < 0.05). The macro-pore frameworks of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP were probably destroyed in acidic or basic solution. Besides, the binding affinity of CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP at pH 7 was lower than that of the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP. Therefore, pH 7 was selected to the optimal solution pH condition.

To ascertain the optimal response time of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP determining DBP, the effect of fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP at different incubation times were shown in Fig. 3c. With the incubation time growing, the fluorescence quenching effect of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP increased gradually and then tended to balance at 1.5 min (p < 0.05). The response time of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was shorter than that of CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP. Additionally, the fluorescence quenching effect of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was higher than that of CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence quenching effect of CdTe@MIP toward DBP was weaker than that of the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP (p < 0.05). Meanwhile, the response time of CdTe@MIP toward DBP was longer than that of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP. These data suggested that the introduction of ZIF-67 shortened the incubation time and enhanced the sensitivity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP. Thus, 1.5 min was selected to optimal response time for further experiments.

Fluorescence emission curves of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP were shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The fluorescence intensities of these two materials decreased with the addition of DBP, but the quenching effect of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was stronger than that of CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP. It was attributed to the specific imprinted sites matched with template molecules in function groups, structure and size existed on the surface of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP, but these imprinted sites absented on the surface of CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP. When the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP was exposed into DBP solution, DBP molecule can be rebound into these imprinted sites.
With the addition of different concentrations of DBP, the fluorescence quenching of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP followed the Stern-Volmer equation (Zhang & Chen, 2016).

F 0/F = 1 + KSV·[DBP]where F0 and F are the original fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP after adding DBP, respectively; KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [DBP] is the concentration of DBP. The Stern-Volmer plots were shown in Fig. 4c and d. The CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP behaved excellent linear fluorescence quenching effect toward DBP in the concentration range of 0.05–18.0 μΜ with a correlation coefficient of 0.998. The linear fluorescence quenching equation of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP was F0 /F = 0.0352[DBP] + 0.0238 with the KSV,MIP of 35190 M−1. And the linear equation of CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP was F0 /F = 0.0142[DBP] + 0.0155 with the KSV,NIP of 14210 M−1. The imprinting factor (IF) which was defined as the ratio of KSV,MIP and KSV,NIP, was used to evaluate the imprinting effect of the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP. According with the KSV,MIP and KSV,NIP, the IF was calculated as 2.48. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 1.6 nM following 3σ/S, which was less than the standards of DBP in drinking water. Where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration.

The anti-interference ability of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP for the recognition of DBP was investigated by comparison the fluorescence quenching effect of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward numerous interfering ions in the present of 0.5 μM of DBP. Besides the relative concentration ratios (CIon/CDBP) of Al3+ and SO4 2- were 2 and 3, the concentration of other ions including Cu2+, Cd2+, Na+, Mn2+, NO3 –, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cl-, Ag+, and K+ were same as DBP. Fig.S5 shows the fluorescence quenching effect of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP and these ions. Compared with the fluorescence quenching effect of DBP, these ions including Cu2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Na+, Mn2+, NO3 –, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cl-, Ag+, SO4 2- and K+ exhibited no apparent influence in the recognition of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP toward DBP (p < 0.05). These data suggested that CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP can be used to detect DBP under tested ions environment.

In order to further assess the specificity of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP, a series of analogues such as DOP, DEP, o-NP, o-CP and o-NA were selected as the competitors. The concentration of analogues and DBP were 0.5 μM. The fluorescence quenching effect of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP and CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP toward DBP, DOP, DEP, o-NP, o-CP and o-NA was displayed in Fig.S6. The results showed the fluorescence intensities of CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP can be quenched by these analogues, but these quenching effects were lower than that of DBP (p < 0.05). The phenomenon was contributed to the imprinted recognition sites on the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP mismatching with these analogues (Lv, Xie, & Zhang, 2018). However, the CdTe@ZIF-67@NIP was unable to detect DBP effectively and specifically.

To verify the practicability of the proposed CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor, different concentrations of DBP added in water, fish, milk and juice samples were tested by standard addition method, and the results were listed in Table 1 . According to the Stern-Volmer plots, the concentrations were calculated. The recoveries of DBP in tap water, barrelled water and bottled water were 98.6–102.4%, 99.3–102.6% and 98.1–106.4%, respectively. The recoveries of DBP in fish, milk and juice samples were 97.2–104.6%, 97.4–101.2% and 98.0%-105.6%, respectively. These results indicated the potential of the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP for determination DBP in real food.

The performance of the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor was compared with previous reported methods for the determination of DBP. As shown in Table 2 , the developed CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor displayed lower determination limit and shorter incubation time compared to other methods. These data exhibited the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor behaved outstanding sensitivity and fast response time in the recognition of DBP.

A new NIR fluorescence imprinted sensor was developed by combing the excellent fluorescence property of NIR CdTe QDs and the high specific surface area of ZIF-67. The CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor behaved outstanding sensitivity and selectivity for rapid recognizing DBP. The sensor exhibited lower LOD of 1.6 nM toward DBP and shorter response time of 1.5 min. Moreover, the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIP sensor was applied successfully to detect DBP in water, fish, milk and juice samples with the recoveries of 97.2–106.4%, which provided a viable method for the rapid determination of DBP in real samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 21565014), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan province (No.2020JJ4500) and the Innovation Fund Designated for Graduate Students of Jishou University (JDY19014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130505.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Chen is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University. Her interest is fluorescence spectral analysis.
Jinli Fu is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University. His interest is fluorescence spectral analysis.
Shu Zhou is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University. Her interest is chromatography analysis.
Pengfei Zhao is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University. His interest is chromatography analysis.
Xiaodan Wu is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University. Her interest is fluorescence spectral analysis.
Sisi Tang is currently a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University. Her interest is fluorescence spectral analysis.

Zhaohui Zhang gained his doctor’s degree from State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University. Nowadays, he is a professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Jishou University, and his research areas are fluorescence spectral and chromatography analysis. In the same time, he is also a member of Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province.
